mechanism: Fluconazole inhibits CYP2C9, the primary enzyme responsible for warfarin metabolism. This reduces warfarin clearance and increases plasma concentrations.
description: Fluconazole increases warfarin plasma concentrations by inhibiting CYP2C9-mediated metabolism. INR can rise significantly, increasing bleeding risk.

mechanism: St. John's Wort induces CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein, increasing the metabolic clearance and reducing therapeutic exposure.
description: St. John's Wort (Hypericum perforatum) induces hepatic CYP3A4 and intestinal P-gp, which may reduce drug effectiveness and glycemic control.

mechanism: Both tramadol (via mu-opioid activity and serotonin reuptake inhibition) and sertraline (SSRI) increase serotonergic neurotransmission.
description: Tramadol has dual mechanisms: opioid agonism and serotonin/norepinephrine reuptake inhibition. Combined with sertraline, the risk of serotonin syndrome increases.

mechanism: Aspirin inhibits platelet aggregation via COX-1 inhibition and can displace warfarin from plasma protein binding sites.
description: The combination of warfarin and aspirin produces additive anticoagulant and antiplatelet effects, significantly increasing bleeding risk.

mechanism: Clarithromycin inhibits CYP3A4, the primary enzyme responsible for simvastatin metabolism. This leads to increased statin plasma concentrations.
description: CYP3A4 inhibition by clarithromycin causes simvastatin accumulation. The risk of myopathy and rhabdomyolysis is significantly increased.
